1:
"And it came to pass after the plague, that the LORD spake unto Moses and unto Eleazar the son of Aaron the priest, saying," The witnesses note a fresh numbering commanded after the plague of Baal-peor, the people to be mustered anew. The witnesses note that Gill notes this was after the plague related in the preceding chapter, perhaps before the war with Midian. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch note the Israelites were to be mustered as the army of Jehovah by a fresh numbering before taking vengeance on Midian. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the after the plague — the judgment of Baal-peor past; the the LORD spake — commanding a new numbering; the to Moses and Eleazar — the old priest gone, the new in place; the the army of Jehovah — counted afresh for the conquest.

The witnesses agree the new census stood at a turning of the history: the plague was over, the old generation all but gone, and Israel was to be numbered again as the host of the LORD on the eve of entering the land; the muster, taken to Moses and Eleazar — not Aaron, now dead — marks the passing of the old and the ordering of the new, the army of God counted afresh for the conquest before them.

So the witnesses make the verse the fresh muster: The witnesses note the after the plague: the judgment of Baal-peor past. The witnesses note the the LORD spake: commanding a new numbering. The witnesses note the to Moses and Eleazar: the old priest gone, the new in place. The witnesses note the the army of Jehovah: counted afresh for the conquest. The expositors observe the after the plague: the judgment of Baal-peor past. The expositors observe the the LORD spake: commanding a new numbering. The expositors observe the to Moses and Eleazar: the old priest gone, the new in place. The expositors observe the the army of Jehovah: counted afresh for the conquest. The believer is taught to mark the after the plague, the the LORD spake, the to Moses and Eleazar, the the army of Jehovah, and to see the hand of God in the numbering of his people. The verse thus records that God commanded a fresh numbering after the plague — Israel mustered anew as the army of the LORD on the eve of the land, the old order passing into the new.
2:
"Take the sum of all the congregation of the children of Israel, from twenty years old and upward, throughout their fathers' house, all that are able to go to war in Israel." The witnesses note the command to number all from twenty up able for war — a count with a double purpose. The witnesses note that Gill notes the Levites were excepted, to be numbered separately; the count was to show that all the old generation that came out of Egypt were now dead, save two, as the Lord had threatened, and that the land might be divided to them. The witnesses note that the muster was of all able to go to war. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the sum of all — from twenty years and upward; the able to go to war — the fighting men mustered; the the old gen dead — proving God's threatening fulfilled; the to divide the land — the tribes counted for inheritance.

The witnesses agree the census served two great ends: to prove the faithfulness of God's threatening — that the murmuring generation had indeed perished in the wilderness, their carcasses fallen as foretold — and to prepare for the dividing of the land, the tribes numbered that each might receive its inheritance; the bare counting thus bore solemn witness both to God's justice in the past and his promise for the future.

So the witnesses make the verse the double purpose: The witnesses note the the sum of all: from twenty years and upward. The witnesses note the able to go to war: the fighting men mustered. The witnesses note the the old gen dead: proving God's threatening fulfilled. The witnesses note the to divide the land: the tribes counted for inheritance. The expositors observe the the sum of all: from twenty years and upward. The expositors observe the able to go to war: the fighting men mustered. The expositors observe the the old gen dead: proving God's threatening fulfilled. The expositors observe the to divide the land: the tribes counted for inheritance. The believer is taught to mark the the sum of all, the able to go to war, the the old gen dead, the to divide the land, and to see the hand of God in the numbering of his people. The verse thus records that the sum was taken of all able for war — proving the old generation dead as God had threatened, and preparing for the dividing of the land among the tribes.
3:
"And Moses and Eleazar the priest spake with them in the plains of Moab by Jordan near Jericho, saying," The witnesses note Moses and Eleazar, with the heads of the tribes, taking the muster in the plains of Moab. The witnesses note that Gill notes they spake with the heads of the people to assist in numbering; the princes who had helped thirty-eight years before were now all dead, and another race succeeded to this service. The witnesses note that the muster was taken by Jordan near Jericho, on the edge of the land. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Moses and Eleazar — taking the muster with the heads; the a new race of princes — the old leaders all dead; the in the plains of Moab — by Jordan near Jericho; the at the threshold — the long wandering all but over.

The witnesses agree the very company of officers preached the passing of a generation: the princes who had numbered Israel at Sinai were dead, and new men stood in their place to take this census — a silent witness that the whole array of leaders had been changed in the wilderness years; and the place, the plains of Moab near Jericho, shows them at last at the threshold of Canaan, the long wandering all but over.

So the witnesses make the verse the new muster-officers: The witnesses note the Moses and Eleazar: taking the muster with the heads. The witnesses note the a new race of princes: the old leaders all dead. The witnesses note the in the plains of Moab: by Jordan near Jericho. The witnesses note the at the threshold: the long wandering all but over. The expositors observe the Moses and Eleazar: taking the muster with the heads. The expositors observe the a new race of princes: the old leaders all dead. The expositors observe the in the plains of Moab: by Jordan near Jericho. The expositors observe the at the threshold: the long wandering all but over. The believer is taught to mark the Moses and Eleazar, the a new race of princes, the in the plains of Moab, the at the threshold, and to see the hand of God in the numbering of his people. The verse thus records that Moses and Eleazar took the muster with a new race of princes — the old leaders dead, Israel now at the threshold of the land by Jordan near Jericho.
4:
"Take the sum of the people, from twenty years old and upward; as the LORD commanded Moses and the children of Israel, which went forth out of the land of Egypt." The witnesses note the muster taken at the same age as the first — from twenty up — marking the change of the generations. The witnesses note that Gill notes it was at the same age as the sum taken thirty-eight years before, so that none could be above sixty of all who went into Canaan, except Joshua and Caleb, and some few in Levi. The witnesses note that the command followed the pattern of the first numbering. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the from twenty years — the same rule as the first muster; the as the LORD commanded — following the former pattern; the none above sixty — the old generation gone; the save Joshua and Caleb — the two faithful alone remaining.

The witnesses agree the repetition of the age-rule throws into relief the wholesale change: counted again from twenty upward, none now stood in the ranks who had been of age at the first muster — save the two faithful spies — for all the rest had died in the wilderness; the bare correspondence of the two censuses is itself a sermon, the new army wholly new, the old fallen to a man but Joshua and Caleb.

So the witnesses make the verse the same age, a new people: The witnesses note the from twenty years: the same rule as the first muster. The witnesses note the as the LORD commanded: following the former pattern. The witnesses note the none above sixty: the old generation gone. The witnesses note the save Joshua and Caleb: the two faithful alone remaining. The expositors observe the from twenty years: the same rule as the first muster. The expositors observe the as the LORD commanded: following the former pattern. The expositors observe the none above sixty: the old generation gone. The expositors observe the save Joshua and Caleb: the two faithful alone remaining. The believer is taught to mark the from twenty years, the as the LORD commanded, the none above sixty, the save Joshua and Caleb, and to see the hand of God in the numbering of his people. The verse thus records that the sum was taken again from twenty up — none of the first muster left save Joshua and Caleb, the new army wholly new, the old fallen to a man.
5:
"Reuben, the eldest son of Israel: the children of Reuben; Hanoch, of whom cometh the family of the Hanochites..." The witnesses note Reuben numbered first as the eldest, his four families, and the tribe decreased since the first muster. The witnesses note that Gill notes Reuben is taken first as Israel's eldest; four families descended from him, and the men fit for war were 43,730, a decrease of 2,770 since the last numbering. The witnesses note that the decrease is in part accounted for by the families of Dathan and Abiram cut off in the rebellion. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the eldest son — reuben counted first; the four families — the houses of his sons; the 43,730 — the men fit for war; the decreased by 2,770 — the loss marked by the rebellion of Dathan and Abiram.

The witnesses agree the decrease of Reuben bore the mark of judgment: the eldest tribe had fallen by some two thousand seven hundred, the loss traced in part to the rebellion of Dathan and Abiram, Reubenites who had perished with Korah and drawn others into their ruin; the diminished number is a quiet record that sin thins the ranks of a people, the rebellious losing not themselves only but the strength of their tribe.

So the witnesses make the verse Reuben numbered first: The witnesses note the the eldest son: Reuben counted first. The witnesses note the four families: the houses of his sons. The witnesses note the 43,730: the men fit for war. The witnesses note the decreased by 2,770: the loss marked by the rebellion of Dathan and Abiram. The expositors observe the the eldest son: Reuben counted first. The expositors observe the four families: the houses of his sons. The expositors observe the 43,730: the men fit for war. The expositors observe the decreased by 2,770: the loss marked by the rebellion of Dathan and Abiram. The believer is taught to mark the the eldest son, the four families, the 43,730, the decreased by 2,770, and to see the hand of God in the numbering of his people. The verse thus records that Reuben, the eldest, numbered 43,730, decreased by 2,770 — the loss marked by the rebellion of Dathan and Abiram, sin thinning the ranks of a tribe.
6:
"Of the children of Reuben (Hezron and Carmi) after their families: ... these are the families... that were numbered of them." The very brief verse records the numbering of the tribe of Reuben (Hezron and Carmi) in the second muster. The witnesses note that Gill and Keil and Delitzsch set the families of Reuben (Hezron and Carmi) and their number, 43,730 in all. The witnesses note that the Hezronites and Carmites complete the four families of Reuben. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the tribe of Reuben (Hezron and Carmi) — reckoned by its families; the 43,730 in all — the sum of its men of war; the the Hezronites and Carmites complete the four fa — the rise or fall since the first muster; the the hand of God — his providence in the numbers.

The witnesses note the bare but weighty record of the new generation's muster: the tribe of Reuben (Hezron and Carmi), with its families, numbered 43,730 in all — and in the rise or fall of the number the hand of God is seen, rewarding the faithful and chastening the rebellious, so that the very census preaches his providence over the increase and decrease of his people.

So the witnesses make the verse the numbering of the tribe: The witnesses note the the tribe of Reuben (Hezron and Carmi): reckoned by its families. The witnesses note the 43,730 in all: the sum of its men of war. The witnesses note the the Hezronites and Carmites complete the four fa: the rise or fall since the first muster. The witnesses note the the hand of God: his providence in the numbers. The expositors observe the the tribe of Reuben (Hezron and Carmi): reckoned by its families. The expositors observe the 43,730 in all: the sum of its men of war. The expositors observe the the Hezronites and Carmites complete the four fa: the rise or fall since the first muster. The expositors observe the the hand of God: his providence in the numbers. The believer is taught to mark the the tribe of Reuben (Hezron and Carmi), the 43,730 in all, the the Hezronites and Carmites complete the four fa, the the hand of God, and to see the hand of God in the numbering of his people. The verse thus records that the tribe of Reuben (Hezron and Carmi) numbered 43,730 in all — the hand of God seen in the change, his providence over the increase and decrease of his people.
7:
"These are the families of the Reubenites: and they that were numbered of them were forty and three thousand and seven hundred and thirty." The witnesses note the sum of Reuben — forty-three thousand seven hundred thirty — a decrease since the first census. The witnesses note that Ellicott notes the decrease of 2,770 compared with the former census (Num. 1:21), Dathan and Abiram having probably enlisted many of their tribe in their rebellion. The witnesses note that Wesley notes the "families" are the chief houses, subdivided into lesser branches. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the 43,730 — the total of the tribe; the a decrease of 2,770 — fewer than at the first muster; the Dathan and Abiram — who enlisted many in their revolt; the a tribe weakened — the followers of rebels falling with them.

The witnesses agree the figure records a real loss, and its cause: Reuben had decreased by 2,770, and the decline is laid to the rebellion of Dathan and Abiram, who had drawn many of their own tribe into the conspiracy against Moses; the diminished total stands as a monument that a tribe is weakened by the sin of its leaders, the followers of rebels falling with them and the whole the poorer for it.

So the witnesses make the verse the Reubenite sum: The witnesses note the 43,730: the total of the tribe. The witnesses note the a decrease of 2,770: fewer than at the first muster. The witnesses note the Dathan and Abiram: who enlisted many in their revolt. The witnesses note the a tribe weakened: the followers of rebels falling with them. The expositors observe the 43,730: the total of the tribe. The expositors observe the a decrease of 2,770: fewer than at the first muster. The expositors observe the Dathan and Abiram: who enlisted many in their revolt. The expositors observe the a tribe weakened: the followers of rebels falling with them. The believer is taught to mark the 43,730, the a decrease of 2,770, the Dathan and Abiram, the a tribe weakened, and to see the hand of God in the numbering of his people. The verse thus records that the Reubenites numbered 43,730, decreased by 2,770 — a monument that the rebellion of Dathan and Abiram weakened the whole tribe.
8:
"And the sons of Pallu; Eliab." The witnesses note the line of Pallu through Eliab — the line in which the rebellion of Dathan and Abiram arose. The witnesses note that Gill notes Pallu had but one son, Eliab, the father of Dathan and Abiram. The witnesses note that Calvin sees the curtailment of the superior line introduced either to exalt God's grace, that Reuben was still numerous despite so great a loss, or to mark the cause of its diminution. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the sons of Pallu; Eliab — the one son, father of the rebels; the the rebel line — where Dathan and Abiram arose; the grace — reuben still numerous despite the loss; the and justice — the judgment falling where the sin was.

The reformed reader (Calvin) draws a double lesson from the naming of this line: the diminished tribe yet remained numerous, which magnifies God's grace in preserving Reuben despite the rebellion; and the singling out of Pallu's line shows the cause of the loss, that the judgment fell where the sin was — grace and justice both legible in the census, the tribe spared yet chastened in the very branch that rebelled.

So the witnesses make the verse the line of Pallu: The witnesses note the sons of Pallu; Eliab: the one son, father of the rebels. The witnesses note the the rebel line: where Dathan and Abiram arose. The witnesses note the grace: Reuben still numerous despite the loss. The witnesses note the and justice: the judgment falling where the sin was. The expositors observe the sons of Pallu; Eliab: the one son, father of the rebels. The expositors observe the the rebel line: where Dathan and Abiram arose. The expositors observe the grace: Reuben still numerous despite the loss. The expositors observe the and justice: the judgment falling where the sin was. The believer is taught to mark the sons of Pallu; Eliab, the the rebel line, the grace, the and justice, and to see the hand of God in the numbering of his people. The verse thus records that Pallu's line through Eliab is named — the branch where the rebellion arose, grace and justice both legible, the tribe spared yet chastened where it sinned.
9:
"And the sons of Eliab; Nemuel, and Dathan, and Abiram. This is that Dathan and Abiram, which were famous in the congregation, who strove against Moses and against Aaron in the company of Korah, when they strove against the LORD:" The witnesses note Dathan and Abiram named again as the rebels who strove against Moses and Aaron with Korah. The witnesses note that Gill notes Nemuel either died without children or perished with his brethren in the conspiracy. The witnesses note that the text recalls that these strove against the LORD himself in the company of Korah. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Dathan and Abiram — the famous rebels recalled; the strove against Moses — and against Aaron with Korah; the against the LORD — their revolt aimed past men to God; the a monument of warning — the sin recorded, not forgotten.

The witnesses agree the census pauses to fasten the memory of the rebellion upon the record: Dathan and Abiram, "famous in the congregation," are named as a warning, their striving against Moses and Aaron declared a striving "against the LORD"; the genealogy turns into a monument of judgment, that the sin of these men should not be forgotten, and that all who rise against God's appointed servants are recorded as rising against God.

So the witnesses make the verse the rebels named: The witnesses note the Dathan and Abiram: the famous rebels recalled. The witnesses note the strove against Moses: and against Aaron with Korah. The witnesses note the against the LORD: their revolt aimed past men to God. The witnesses note the a monument of warning: the sin recorded, not forgotten. The expositors observe the Dathan and Abiram: the famous rebels recalled. The expositors observe the strove against Moses: and against Aaron with Korah. The expositors observe the against the LORD: their revolt aimed past men to God. The expositors observe the a monument of warning: the sin recorded, not forgotten. The believer is taught to mark the Dathan and Abiram, the strove against Moses, the against the LORD, the a monument of warning, and to see the hand of God in the numbering of his people. The verse thus records that Dathan and Abiram are named as those who strove against Moses, Aaron, and the LORD — the census turned to a monument of warning against rebellion.
10:
"And the earth opened her mouth, and swallowed them up together with Korah, when that company died, what time the fire devoured two hundred and fifty men: and they became a sign." The witnesses note the recalling of the judgment — the earth swallowing the rebels, the fire devouring the 250 — that they became a sign. The witnesses note that Gill notes the earth swallowed up Dathan and Abiram, their wives, children, houses, and goods, together with Korah's company, while the fire devoured the 250. The witnesses note that they "became a sign" — a warning to others. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the earth swallowed them — the rebels taken down alive; the the fire devoured — the 250 censer-bearers consumed; the with Korah — the whole company judged; the a perpetual sign — the judgment set up as a warning.

The witnesses agree the census preserves the judgment as a perpetual sign: the swallowing of the rebels and the burning of the 250 are recalled in the very record of the tribes, that the fate of those who defied God might stand as a lasting warning; the phrase "they became a sign" shows that God turns his judgments into instruction, the destruction of the rebellious set up as a beacon to deter the living from the like sin.

So the witnesses make the verse they became a sign: The witnesses note the the earth swallowed them: the rebels taken down alive. The witnesses note the the fire devoured: the 250 censer-bearers consumed. The witnesses note the with Korah: the whole company judged. The witnesses note the a perpetual sign: the judgment set up as a warning. The expositors observe the the earth swallowed them: the rebels taken down alive. The expositors observe the the fire devoured: the 250 censer-bearers consumed. The expositors observe the with Korah: the whole company judged. The expositors observe the a perpetual sign: the judgment set up as a warning. The believer is taught to mark the the earth swallowed them, the the fire devoured, the with Korah, the a perpetual sign, and to see the hand of God in the numbering of his people. The verse thus records that the swallowing of the rebels and the fire on the 250 are recalled — God turning his judgments into a perpetual sign, the destruction of the rebellious a warning to the living.
11:
"Notwithstanding the children of Korah died not." The witnesses note the bright note of mercy — though Korah perished, his children did not die. The witnesses note that Gill notes they died not by pestilence, fire, or the swallowing earth, not being in their father's counsel, but following the doctrine of Moses, as the Targum says; they either disliked the scheme or repented and departed from it. The witnesses note that the children of Korah were spared. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the died not — spared amid the judgment; the not in his counsel — not partakers of the father's sin; the mercy to the children — those who would not follow the rebel; the turned to praise — their sons later singers in God's house (Ps. 42).

The witnesses agree this brief verse shines with grace amid the record of judgment: the sons of Korah, though their father led the great rebellion, did not perish with him, for they were not partakers in his sin; the mercy that spares the children who will not follow the father's rebellion is a precious comfort — and these very sons of Korah became, in after times, singers in the house of God and authors of psalms (cf. the titles of Psalms 42), the saved seed of a rebel turned to the praise of the LORD.

So the witnesses make the verse the children of Korah: The witnesses note the died not: spared amid the judgment. The witnesses note the not in his counsel: not partakers of the father's sin. The witnesses note the mercy to the children: those who would not follow the rebel. The witnesses note the turned to praise: their sons later singers in God's house (Ps. 42). The expositors observe the died not: spared amid the judgment. The expositors observe the not in his counsel: not partakers of the father's sin. The expositors observe the mercy to the children: those who would not follow the rebel. The expositors observe the turned to praise: their sons later singers in God's house (Ps. 42). The believer is taught to mark the died not, the not in his counsel, the mercy to the children, the turned to praise, and to see the hand of God in the numbering of his people. The verse thus records that the children of Korah died not — grace sparing the children who would not follow the father's rebellion, their seed later singers of the LORD's praise (Ps. 42).
12:
"The sons of Simeon after their families: of Nemuel, the family of the Nemuelites: of Jamin, the family of the Jaminites..." The witnesses note the tribe of Simeon numbered next, under Reuben's standard, with some variation in the names of its families. The witnesses note that Gill notes Simeon was numbered next, being under the standard of Reuben; two of his sons are named a little differently than in Genesis 46:10, and one (Ohad) is omitted, perhaps dying without children. The witnesses note that the families are reckoned by his sons. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the next to Reuben — numbered under his standard; the altered names — the sons named a little differently (Gen. 46:10); the Ohad omitted — a line failed for want of children; the houses rise and fall — providence ordering the families.

The witnesses agree the small differences in the family names mark the passing of time and the dying out of branches: one of Simeon's sons is omitted, his line failing for want of children, and others appear under altered names; the genealogy quietly records how families rise and fall across the generations, some continued and some cut off, the providence of God ordering the increase and the extinction of the houses of his people.

So the witnesses make the verse the families of Simeon: The witnesses note the next to Reuben: numbered under his standard. The witnesses note the altered names: the sons named a little differently (Gen. 46:10). The witnesses note the Ohad omitted: a line failed for want of children. The witnesses note the houses rise and fall: providence ordering the families. The expositors observe the next to Reuben: numbered under his standard. The expositors observe the altered names: the sons named a little differently (Gen. 46:10). The expositors observe the Ohad omitted: a line failed for want of children. The expositors observe the houses rise and fall: providence ordering the families. The believer is taught to mark the next to Reuben, the altered names, the Ohad omitted, the houses rise and fall, and to see the hand of God in the numbering of his people. The verse thus records that Simeon was numbered next, a son omitted and names altered — the providence of God ordering the rise and extinction of the houses of his people across the generations.
13:
"Of the children of Simeon (Zerah and Shaul) after their families: ... these are the families... that were numbered of them." The very brief verse records the numbering of the tribe of Simeon (Zerah and Shaul) in the second muster. The witnesses note that Gill and Keil and Delitzsch set the families of Simeon (Zerah and Shaul) and their number, 22,200 in all. The witnesses note that the family of the Zarhites and the Shaulites complete the tribe of Simeon. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the tribe of Simeon (Zerah and Shaul) — reckoned by its families; the 22,200 in all — the sum of its men of war; the the family of the Zarhites and the Shaulites com — the rise or fall since the first muster; the the hand of God — his providence in the numbers.

The witnesses note the bare but weighty record of the new generation's muster: the tribe of Simeon (Zerah and Shaul), with its families, numbered 22,200 in all — and in the rise or fall of the number the hand of God is seen, rewarding the faithful and chastening the rebellious, so that the very census preaches his providence over the increase and decrease of his people.

So the witnesses make the verse the numbering of the tribe: The witnesses note the the tribe of Simeon (Zerah and Shaul): reckoned by its families. The witnesses note the 22,200 in all: the sum of its men of war. The witnesses note the the family of the Zarhites and the Shaulites com: the rise or fall since the first muster. The witnesses note the the hand of God: his providence in the numbers. The expositors observe the the tribe of Simeon (Zerah and Shaul): reckoned by its families. The expositors observe the 22,200 in all: the sum of its men of war. The expositors observe the the family of the Zarhites and the Shaulites com: the rise or fall since the first muster. The expositors observe the the hand of God: his providence in the numbers. The believer is taught to mark the the tribe of Simeon (Zerah and Shaul), the 22,200 in all, the the family of the Zarhites and the Shaulites com, the the hand of God, and to see the hand of God in the numbering of his people. The verse thus records that the tribe of Simeon (Zerah and Shaul) numbered 22,200 in all — the hand of God seen in the change, his providence over the increase and decrease of his people.
14:
"These are the families of the Simeonites, twenty and two thousand and two hundred." The witnesses note the great decrease of Simeon — from fifty-nine thousand to twenty-two thousand two hundred. The witnesses note that Ellicott notes a decrease of 37,100 in Simeon; Zimri, the chief offender in the matter of Baal-peor, belonged to this tribe, and probably led many of his tribe astray with him. The witnesses note that this is the only tribe on which Moses pronounced no blessing (Deut. 33). The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the 22,200 — the sum of Simeon now; the a decrease of 37,100 — the heaviest fall of all the tribes; the Zimri of Simeon — the chief offender at Baal-peor; the no blessing — the only tribe Moses left unblessed (Deut. 33).

The witnesses agree Simeon's vast decline is the plainest mark of judgment in the whole census: the tribe had lost more than thirty-seven thousand men, the heaviest fall of all, and the cause is traced to Baal-peor, where Zimri of Simeon led the way into sin and many of his tribe with him; the diminished number, and the silence of Moses' blessing over Simeon alone, stand together as a solemn witness that the sin of Baal-peor reaped a terrible harvest, the tribe most given to that sin most wasted by its judgment.

So the witnesses make the verse the fall of Simeon: The witnesses note the 22,200: the sum of Simeon now. The witnesses note the a decrease of 37,100: the heaviest fall of all the tribes. The witnesses note the Zimri of Simeon: the chief offender at Baal-peor. The witnesses note the no blessing: the only tribe Moses left unblessed (Deut. 33). The expositors observe the 22,200: the sum of Simeon now. The expositors observe the a decrease of 37,100: the heaviest fall of all the tribes. The expositors observe the Zimri of Simeon: the chief offender at Baal-peor. The expositors observe the no blessing: the only tribe Moses left unblessed (Deut. 33). The believer is taught to mark the 22,200, the a decrease of 37,100, the Zimri of Simeon, the no blessing, and to see the hand of God in the numbering of his people. The verse thus records that Simeon fell to 22,200, a decrease of 37,100 — the heaviest of all, traced to Baal-peor where Zimri led the tribe into sin, the tribe most given to that sin most wasted by its judgment.
15:
"The children of Gad after their families: of Zephon, the family of the Zephonites: of Haggi, the family of the Haggites..." The witnesses note Gad numbered next, under Reuben's standard, with some variation in its family names and a decrease. The witnesses note that Gill notes Gad was numbered next to Simeon, encamped under the standard of Reuben; some names vary from Genesis 46:16; seven families sprang from Gad, now numbering 40,500, diminished since the first muster. The witnesses note that the families are reckoned by his sons. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the next to Simeon — under Reuben's standard; the seven families — reckoned by his sons (Gen. 46:16); the 40,500 — the men of war, diminished; the the camp diminished — the south-side tribes declining together.

The witnesses agree Gad, like the tribes camped with it, shows a decline: its seven families numbered fewer than before, and the three tribes under Reuben's standard — Reuben, Simeon, and Gad — were all diminished together; the common loss of the camp that lay on the south side hints that they had shared a common contagion of sin, the neighbouring tribes drawn down together, the census recording how evil example spreads its harm through a whole quarter of the host.

So the witnesses make the verse the tribe of Gad: The witnesses note the next to Simeon: under Reuben's standard. The witnesses note the seven families: reckoned by his sons (Gen. 46:16). The witnesses note the 40,500: the men of war, diminished. The witnesses note the the camp diminished: the south-side tribes declining together. The expositors observe the next to Simeon: under Reuben's standard. The expositors observe the seven families: reckoned by his sons (Gen. 46:16). The expositors observe the 40,500: the men of war, diminished. The expositors observe the the camp diminished: the south-side tribes declining together. The believer is taught to mark the next to Simeon, the seven families, the 40,500, the the camp diminished, and to see the hand of God in the numbering of his people. The verse thus records that Gad numbered 40,500, diminished — the tribes of Reuben's standard declining together, evil example spreading its harm through a whole quarter of the host.
16:
"Of Ozni, the family of the Oznites: of Eri, the family of the Erites:" The very brief verse continues the families of Gad — the Oznites and the Erites. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the families of Ozni and Eri among the seven of Gad. The witnesses note that Ozni answers to Ezbon of the earlier list (Gen. 46:16). The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the Oznites — ozni, answering to Ezbon (Gen. 46:16); the the Erites — another of Gad's seven families; the every house reckoned — the muster complete and exact; the the names shifting — a little altered across the generations; the the line preserved — the families faithfully traced by God's providence.

The witnesses note the careful continuing of the family list: each branch of Gad is named in its turn, the Oznites and Erites among them, that the muster be complete and every house reckoned; the small variation of "Ozni" for the earlier "Ezbon" shows the names shifting a little across the generations, yet the line is faithfully traced, the providence of God preserving the record of each family of his people, that none of the houses of Israel be lost from the account, but each have its place in the numbering and, in time, its portion in the land.

So the witnesses make the verse the families of Gad continued: The witnesses note the the Oznites: Ozni, answering to Ezbon (Gen. 46:16). The witnesses note the the Erites: another of Gad's seven families. The witnesses note the every house reckoned: the muster complete and exact. The witnesses note the the names shifting: a little altered across the generations. The witnesses note the the line preserved: the families faithfully traced by God's providence. The expositors observe the the Oznites: Ozni, answering to Ezbon (Gen. 46:16). The expositors observe the the Erites: another of Gad's seven families. The expositors observe the every house reckoned: the muster complete and exact. The expositors observe the the names shifting: a little altered across the generations. The expositors observe the the line preserved: the families faithfully traced by God's providence. The believer is taught to mark the the Oznites, the the Erites, the every house reckoned, the the names shifting, the the line preserved, and to see the hand of God in the numbering of his people. The verse thus records that the Oznites and Erites continue the families of Gad — every house reckoned, the line faithfully traced across the generations.
18:
"These are the families of the children of Gad according to those that were numbered of them, forty thousand and five hundred." The witnesses note the sum of Gad — forty thousand five hundred — a decrease of more than five thousand. The witnesses note that Ellicott notes a decrease of 5,150; Reuben, Simeon, and Gad encamped together on the south of the tabernacle, and had probably been mutually contaminated by each other's evil example. The witnesses note that the families of Gad are reckoned in this sum. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the 40,500 — the total of Gad; the a decrease of 5,150 — fewer than at the first muster; the the southern camp — reuben, Simeon, and Gad together; the mutual contamination — evil example spreading from tribe to tribe.

The witnesses agree the decline of Gad confirms the pattern of the southern camp: like Reuben and Simeon beside it, Gad had decreased, the three tribes that pitched together on the south side all the poorer at the second muster; Ellicott draws the moral that they had "mutually contaminated" one another, the sin of evil company spreading from tribe to neighbouring tribe — a warning that the ungodly example of those nearest us draws us into a shared loss.

So the witnesses make the verse the Gadite sum: The witnesses note the 40,500: the total of Gad. The witnesses note the a decrease of 5,150: fewer than at the first muster. The witnesses note the the southern camp: Reuben, Simeon, and Gad together. The witnesses note the mutual contamination: evil example spreading from tribe to tribe. The expositors observe the 40,500: the total of Gad. The expositors observe the a decrease of 5,150: fewer than at the first muster. The expositors observe the the southern camp: Reuben, Simeon, and Gad together. The expositors observe the mutual contamination: evil example spreading from tribe to tribe. The believer is taught to mark the 40,500, the a decrease of 5,150, the the southern camp, the mutual contamination, and to see the hand of God in the numbering of his people. The verse thus records that Gad numbered 40,500, decreased by 5,150 — the southern camp diminished together, the ungodly example of neighbours drawing them into a shared loss.
19:
"The sons of Judah were Er and Onan: and Er and Onan died in the land of Canaan." The witnesses note the tribe of Judah, beginning with Er and Onan, who died in Canaan without offspring. The witnesses note that Gill notes Judah had also Shelah, Pharez, and Zerah, from whom families sprang, but none from Er and Onan. The witnesses note that Ellicott and Calvin recall that Er and Onan died in the land of Canaan (Gen. 38:7-10); Calvin magnifies God's grace that, of the three sons left to Judah, the tribe grew so numerous. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Er and Onan — the first sons, who died childless; the died in Canaan — under God's judgment (Gen. 38:7-10); the the remaining line — shelah, Pharez, and Zerah; the God's grace magnified — a great tribe from a reduced stock.

The reformed reader (Calvin) turns the death of Er and Onan into a magnifying of God's grace: two of Judah's sons died childless under God's judgment, yet from the remaining line the tribe grew to be the largest of all — a proof that the increase of God's people rests not on the strength of nature but on his blessing, who could make a great tribe spring from a stock so reduced, and raise the line of promise, and of Christ, out of so unpromising a beginning.

So the witnesses make the verse the sons of Judah: The witnesses note the Er and Onan: the first sons, who died childless. The witnesses note the died in Canaan: under God's judgment (Gen. 38:7-10). The witnesses note the the remaining line: Shelah, Pharez, and Zerah. The witnesses note the God's grace magnified: a great tribe from a reduced stock. The expositors observe the Er and Onan: the first sons, who died childless. The expositors observe the died in Canaan: under God's judgment (Gen. 38:7-10). The expositors observe the the remaining line: Shelah, Pharez, and Zerah. The expositors observe the God's grace magnified: a great tribe from a reduced stock. The believer is taught to mark the Er and Onan, the died in Canaan, the the remaining line, the God's grace magnified, and to see the hand of God in the numbering of his people. The verse thus records that Er and Onan died childless, yet from Judah's remaining line the great tribe sprang — magnifying God's grace, the increase of his people resting on his blessing, not nature's strength.
20:
"And the sons of Judah after their families were; of Shelah, the family of the Shelanites: of Pharez, the family of the Pharzites: of Zerah, the family of the Zarhites." The witnesses note the families of Judah — the Shelanites, Pharzites, and Zarhites — the largest of the tribes. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the families of Judah by Shelah, Pharez, and Zerah, with the sons of Pharez, Hezron and Hamul. The witnesses note that Judah numbered threescore and sixteen thousand five hundred (76,500). The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Shelah, Pharez, Zerah — the three lines of Judah; the the largest tribe — the foremost in number; the the line of Pharez — through Hezron and Hamul, the line of promise; the the tribe of the King — from which David and Christ should come (Gen. 49:10).

The witnesses agree Judah stands out as the foremost tribe in number as in dignity: from Shelah, Pharez, and Zerah sprang its families, the largest muster of all the tribes, fulfilling the patriarchal word that the sceptre should not depart from Judah; the line of Pharez — through Hezron and Hamul — is the very line of the promise, from which David and at last the Christ should come, so that the greatness of Judah's number befits the tribe of the coming King (cf. Gen. 49:10).

So the witnesses make the verse the families of Judah: The witnesses note the Shelah, Pharez, Zerah: the three lines of Judah. The witnesses note the the largest tribe: the foremost in number. The witnesses note the the line of Pharez: through Hezron and Hamul, the line of promise. The witnesses note the the tribe of the King: from which David and Christ should come (Gen. 49:10). The expositors observe the Shelah, Pharez, Zerah: the three lines of Judah. The expositors observe the the largest tribe: the foremost in number. The expositors observe the the line of Pharez: through Hezron and Hamul, the line of promise. The expositors observe the the tribe of the King: from which David and Christ should come (Gen. 49:10). The believer is taught to mark the Shelah, Pharez, Zerah, the the largest tribe, the the line of Pharez, the the tribe of the King, and to see the hand of God in the numbering of his people. The verse thus records that the families of Judah are named — the largest tribe, the line of Pharez the line of promise, befitting the tribe of the coming King (Gen. 49:10).
21:
"And the sons of Pharez were; of Hezron, the family of the Hezronites: of Hamul, the family of the Hamulites." The witnesses note the sons of Pharez — Hezron and Hamul — substituted in the place of the childless Er and Onan. The witnesses note that Ellicott notes Judah had five sons, but inasmuch as Er and Onan died childless, Hezron and Hamul were substituted in their place (Gen. 46:12). The witnesses note that the families of Judah are reckoned through these. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Hezron and Hamul — the sons of Pharez; the substituted — for the childless Er and Onan (Gen. 46:12); the the count complete — the tribe losing nothing of its number; the the line of David — hezron in the genealogy of Christ (Matt. 1:3).

The witnesses agree the substitution of Pharez's sons for the dead is full of quiet significance: where two of Judah's sons had perished without seed, the grandsons Hezron and Hamul took their place in the count, so that the tribe lost nothing of its complement; and Hezron stands in the very genealogy of David and of Christ (Ruth 4:18; Matt. 1:3) — the line of promise carried on through the substituted heirs, God's purpose unbroken though death thinned the family.

So the witnesses make the verse the sons of Pharez: The witnesses note the Hezron and Hamul: the sons of Pharez. The witnesses note the substituted: for the childless Er and Onan (Gen. 46:12). The witnesses note the the count complete: the tribe losing nothing of its number. The witnesses note the the line of David: Hezron in the genealogy of Christ (Matt. 1:3). The expositors observe the Hezron and Hamul: the sons of Pharez. The expositors observe the substituted: for the childless Er and Onan (Gen. 46:12). The expositors observe the the count complete: the tribe losing nothing of its number. The expositors observe the the line of David: Hezron in the genealogy of Christ (Matt. 1:3). The believer is taught to mark the Hezron and Hamul, the substituted, the the count complete, the the line of David, and to see the hand of God in the numbering of his people. The verse thus records that Hezron and Hamul were reckoned in place of the childless Er and Onan — the line of promise carried on through the substituted heirs, Hezron in the line of David and Christ (Matt. 1:3).
22:
"These are the families of Judah according to those that were numbered of them, threescore and sixteen thousand and five hundred." The witnesses note the sum of Judah — seventy-six thousand five hundred — increased, while the foregoing tribes were all diminished. The witnesses note that Wesley notes the families of Judah were about two thousand more than they were (Num. 1:27), whereas the foregoing tribes were all diminished. The witnesses note that Judah was the largest of all the tribes. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the 76,500 — the sum of Judah, the largest; the increased — about two thousand more (Num. 1:27); the against the others — while Reuben, Simeon, Gad diminished; the God's favour — blessing on Judah, judgment on the rebellious.

The witnesses agree the increase of Judah, set against the decrease of Reuben, Simeon, and Gad, shows the hand of God blessing where the others were chastened: the tribe of the coming King grew larger while the rebellious tribes shrank, the census marking out God's favour upon Judah; the contrast preaches that the LORD adds to those who keep their place and follow him, and diminishes those who rebel — the rise and fall of the tribes a register of his blessing and his judgment.

So the witnesses make the verse the increase of Judah: The witnesses note the 76,500: the sum of Judah, the largest. The witnesses note the increased: about two thousand more (Num. 1:27). The witnesses note the against the others: while Reuben, Simeon, Gad diminished. The witnesses note the God's favour: blessing on Judah, judgment on the rebellious. The expositors observe the 76,500: the sum of Judah, the largest. The expositors observe the increased: about two thousand more (Num. 1:27). The expositors observe the against the others: while Reuben, Simeon, Gad diminished. The expositors observe the God's favour: blessing on Judah, judgment on the rebellious. The believer is taught to mark the 76,500, the increased, the against the others, the God's favour, and to see the hand of God in the numbering of his people. The verse thus records that Judah increased to 76,500 while the foregoing tribes diminished — the hand of God blessing the tribe of the King, the rise and fall of the tribes a register of his favour and judgment.
23:
"Of the sons of Issachar after their families: of Tola, the family of the Tolaites: of Pua, the family of the Punites:" The witnesses note Issachar numbered next, under Judah's standard, its four families increased by nearly ten thousand. The witnesses note that Gill notes Issachar was numbered next to Judah, being under his standard; two sons are named a little differently (Pua for Phuvah, Jashub for Job); four families, the warlike men 64,300, increased by 9,900 since the last muster. The witnesses note that the increase was great. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the next to Judah — under his standard; the four families — reckoned by his sons; the 64,300 — the warlike men; the increased by 9,900 — the blessing of God on the tribe.

The witnesses agree Issachar, like Judah whose standard it followed, shows the blessing of God in a large increase: its four families had grown by nearly ten thousand men, a marked contrast to the diminished tribes of the south; the prospering of the tribes that kept their order and followed the standard of Judah confirms that God multiplies those who walk in his way, the census again recording the difference his favour makes between the faithful and the rebellious.

So the witnesses make the verse the increase of Issachar: The witnesses note the next to Judah: under his standard. The witnesses note the four families: reckoned by his sons. The witnesses note the 64,300: the warlike men. The witnesses note the increased by 9,900: the blessing of God on the tribe. The expositors observe the next to Judah: under his standard. The expositors observe the four families: reckoned by his sons. The expositors observe the 64,300: the warlike men. The expositors observe the increased by 9,900: the blessing of God on the tribe. The believer is taught to mark the next to Judah, the four families, the 64,300, the increased by 9,900, and to see the hand of God in the numbering of his people. The verse thus records that Issachar increased to 64,300, up by nearly ten thousand — the blessing of God on the tribe that followed Judah's standard, multiplying those who walk in his way.
24:
"Of Jashub, the family of the Jashubites: of Shimron, the family of the Shimronites." The very brief verse completes the families of Issachar — the Jashubites and Shimronites. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the families of Jashub and Shimron, completing the four of Issachar, numbered 64,300. The witnesses note that the tribe is reckoned by these. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the Jashubites — one of Issachar's houses; the the Shimronites — completing the four families; the 64,300 — the increased number gathered; the none overlooked — every branch named in its order; the the increase God's gift — the growth no chance of nature but his blessing.

The witnesses note the completing of Issachar's families: the Jashubites and Shimronites finish the four houses of the tribe, every branch named in its order, the increased number gathered under its families; the careful reckoning shows again that God numbers his people by their houses, none overlooked, the growth of the tribe registered family by family as a record of his blessing upon them. The very particularity of the list, naming each house though it adds but a name, witnesses that the LORD knows his people individually, and that the increase of a tribe is no chance of nature but the gift of the God who multiplies and preserves the families of his own, that each may have its portion in the inheritance to come.

So the witnesses make the verse the families completed: The witnesses note the the Jashubites: one of Issachar's houses. The witnesses note the the Shimronites: completing the four families. The witnesses note the 64,300: the increased number gathered. The witnesses note the none overlooked: every branch named in its order. The witnesses note the the increase God's gift: the growth no chance of nature but his blessing. The expositors observe the the Jashubites: one of Issachar's houses. The expositors observe the the Shimronites: completing the four families. The expositors observe the 64,300: the increased number gathered. The expositors observe the none overlooked: every branch named in its order. The expositors observe the the increase God's gift: the growth no chance of nature but his blessing. The believer is taught to mark the the Jashubites, the the Shimronites, the 64,300, the none overlooked, the the increase God's gift, and to see the hand of God in the numbering of his people. The verse thus records that the Jashubites and Shimronites complete Issachar's families — every branch named, the increased tribe registered house by house as a record of God's blessing, who knows and multiplies his people's families.
26:
"Of the sons of Zebulun after their families: of Sered, the family of the Sardites: of Elon, the family of the Elonites: of Jahleel, the family of the Jahleelites." The witnesses note Zebulun numbered under Judah's standard, its three families increased. The witnesses note that Gill notes Zebulun was numbered next to Issachar, encamped under the standard of Judah; it consisted of three families, numbering 60,500, increased by 3,100. The witnesses note that the families are reckoned by his sons. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the next to Issachar — under Judah's standard; the three families — reckoned by his sons; the 60,500 — the men of war; the increased by 3,100 — the camp of Judah prospering together.

The witnesses agree Zebulun, the third tribe under Judah's standard, likewise increased: its three families had grown by some three thousand, so that all the tribes of Judah's camp — Judah, Issachar, and Zebulun — prospered together, even as the tribes of Reuben's camp had all declined; the parallel is striking, the eastern camp of Judah blessed and the southern camp of Reuben diminished, the census setting the obedient and the rebellious side by side in their increase and decrease.

So the witnesses make the verse the increase of Zebulun: The witnesses note the next to Issachar: under Judah's standard. The witnesses note the three families: reckoned by his sons. The witnesses note the 60,500: the men of war. The witnesses note the increased by 3,100: the camp of Judah prospering together. The expositors observe the next to Issachar: under Judah's standard. The expositors observe the three families: reckoned by his sons. The expositors observe the 60,500: the men of war. The expositors observe the increased by 3,100: the camp of Judah prospering together. The believer is taught to mark the next to Issachar, the three families, the 60,500, the increased by 3,100, and to see the hand of God in the numbering of his people. The verse thus records that Zebulun increased to 60,500 — the camp of Judah prospering together, set against the diminished camp of Reuben, the obedient and rebellious side by side.
27:
"These are the families of the Zebulunites according to those that were numbered of them, threescore thousand and five hundred." The witnesses note the sum of Zebulun — sixty thousand five hundred — and the turn now to the sons of Joseph. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the Zebulunites at 60,500, and then the sons of Joseph, Manasseh and Ephraim, classified in two leading families. The witnesses note that Joseph's descendants were reckoned by his two sons, raised into founders of tribes. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the 60,500 — the sum of Zebulun; the the sons of Joseph — manasseh and Ephraim; the two leading families — raised into founders of tribes; the Joseph's double portion — his grandsons adopted as tribes (Gen. 48:5).

The witnesses agree the sum closes the camp of Judah and opens the house of Joseph: Zebulun stood at 60,500, and the count turns to Manasseh and Ephraim, the two sons of Joseph whom Jacob had raised to be founders of tribes in their own right; the doubling of Joseph into two tribes, fulfilling Jacob's adoption of his grandsons (Gen. 48:5), shows the honour set on Joseph, his single portion become a double inheritance among the tribes of Israel.

So the witnesses make the verse Zebulun and Joseph: The witnesses note the 60,500: the sum of Zebulun. The witnesses note the the sons of Joseph: Manasseh and Ephraim. The witnesses note the two leading families: raised into founders of tribes. The witnesses note the Joseph's double portion: his grandsons adopted as tribes (Gen. 48:5). The expositors observe the 60,500: the sum of Zebulun. The expositors observe the the sons of Joseph: Manasseh and Ephraim. The expositors observe the two leading families: raised into founders of tribes. The expositors observe the Joseph's double portion: his grandsons adopted as tribes (Gen. 48:5). The believer is taught to mark the 60,500, the the sons of Joseph, the two leading families, the Joseph's double portion, and to see the hand of God in the numbering of his people. The verse thus records that Zebulun numbered 60,500, and the count turns to Joseph's two sons — Manasseh and Ephraim made founders of tribes, Joseph's single portion become a double inheritance (Gen. 48:5).
28:
"The sons of Joseph after their families were Manasseh and Ephraim." The witnesses note the two tribes of Joseph — Manasseh named first, though Ephraim was preferred by Jacob. The witnesses note that Gill notes Manasseh is mentioned first, though Ephraim was preferred by Jacob and had the standard, not because he was firstborn but because he had now the greater increase, having one son Machir and a grandson Gilead, whose family was large. The witnesses note that the families of Joseph are reckoned through his two sons. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Manasseh and Ephraim — the two tribes of Joseph; the Manasseh first — for his greater present increase; the through Machir and Gilead — his line grown into many families; the Jacob's word — the younger to be greater in the end (Gen. 48:19).

The witnesses agree the order of naming reflects the present growth, not the patriarchal blessing: Manasseh, though Jacob set Ephraim before him, is named first here because his tribe had now multiplied the more, his line through Machir and Gilead grown into many families; the census follows the actual increase God had given, yet the standing word of Jacob — that the younger should be greater (Gen. 48:19) — would hold in the end, the two tribes of Joseph alike honoured in Israel.

So the witnesses make the verse the sons of Joseph: The witnesses note the Manasseh and Ephraim: the two tribes of Joseph. The witnesses note the Manasseh first: for his greater present increase. The witnesses note the through Machir and Gilead: his line grown into many families. The witnesses note the Jacob's word: the younger to be greater in the end (Gen. 48:19). The expositors observe the Manasseh and Ephraim: the two tribes of Joseph. The expositors observe the Manasseh first: for his greater present increase. The expositors observe the through Machir and Gilead: his line grown into many families. The expositors observe the Jacob's word: the younger to be greater in the end (Gen. 48:19). The believer is taught to mark the Manasseh and Ephraim, the Manasseh first, the through Machir and Gilead, the Jacob's word, and to see the hand of God in the numbering of his people. The verse thus records that Manasseh is named first for his present increase, though Ephraim was preferred by Jacob — the census following the growth God gave, Jacob's word yet to hold (Gen. 48:19).
29:
"Of the sons of Manasseh: of Machir, the family of the Machirites: and Machir begat Gilead: of Gilead come the family of the Gileadites." The witnesses note the families of Manasseh — eight in all, springing from Machir, Gilead, and Gilead's six sons. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch note eight families descended from Manasseh: one from Machir, the second from Gilead, and six from the sons of Gilead, the genealogies of Num. 27 and 36 and Joshua 17 harmonizing with this. The witnesses note that the line runs Machir to Gilead to his sons. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Machir — the son from whom the line runs; the Gilead — machir's son, father of six; the eight families — the houses of Manasseh; the preparing for the law — the line leading toward Zelophehad and inheritance (Num. 27).

The witnesses agree the careful tracing of Manasseh's line through Machir and Gilead prepares for what follows: the naming of Gilead's sons, and presently of Zelophehad among them, sets the stage for the question of inheritance that the daughters of Zelophehad would raise; the genealogy is not idle, but lays the ground for the law of inheritance to come, the providence of God ordering even the family-lists toward the just dividing of the land.

So the witnesses make the verse the families of Manasseh: The witnesses note the Machir: the son from whom the line runs. The witnesses note the Gilead: Machir's son, father of six. The witnesses note the eight families: the houses of Manasseh. The witnesses note the preparing for the law: the line leading toward Zelophehad and inheritance (Num. 27). The expositors observe the Machir: the son from whom the line runs. The expositors observe the Gilead: Machir's son, father of six. The expositors observe the eight families: the houses of Manasseh. The expositors observe the preparing for the law: the line leading toward Zelophehad and inheritance (Num. 27). The believer is taught to mark the Machir, the Gilead, the eight families, the preparing for the law, and to see the hand of God in the numbering of his people. The verse thus records that Manasseh's eight families spring from Machir and Gilead — the line laying the ground for the question of inheritance the daughters of Zelophehad would raise (Num. 27).
30:
"These are the sons of Gilead: of Jeezer, the family of the Jeezerites: of Helek, the family of the Helekites:" The very brief verse names the sons of Gilead — the houses of Manasseh through whom the tribe is reckoned. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the sons of Gilead — Jeezer, Helek, and the rest — as the six families that, with Machir and Gilead, make up the eight of Manasseh. The witnesses note that among these sons stood Hepher, whose son Zelophehad had only daughters. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Jeezer, Helek — the first of Gilead's sons; the the six families — completing the houses of Manasseh; the the line of Hepher — from which Zelophehad came; the pointing forward — the root of the daughters' inheritance case.

The witnesses note the naming of Gilead's sons completes the house of Manasseh and points forward: among these families is the line of Hepher, from which came Zelophehad, who had no sons but daughters — so that this dry list of names is the very root of the famous case of the daughters' inheritance; the census, in tracing each family, quietly prepares the ground on which a question of justice and a new law would soon be raised.

So the witnesses make the verse the sons of Gilead: The witnesses note the Jeezer, Helek: the first of Gilead's sons. The witnesses note the the six families: completing the houses of Manasseh. The witnesses note the the line of Hepher: from which Zelophehad came. The witnesses note the pointing forward: the root of the daughters' inheritance case. The expositors observe the Jeezer, Helek: the first of Gilead's sons. The expositors observe the the six families: completing the houses of Manasseh. The expositors observe the the line of Hepher: from which Zelophehad came. The expositors observe the pointing forward: the root of the daughters' inheritance case. The believer is taught to mark the Jeezer, Helek, the the six families, the the line of Hepher, the pointing forward, and to see the hand of God in the numbering of his people. The verse thus records that the sons of Gilead are named, completing Manasseh's houses — among them the line of Hepher and Zelophehad, the root of the famous case of the daughters' inheritance.
